section.title: Limits in several variables
section.p: gave us graphs, level curves, and level surfaces. Those pictures help us see scalar fields, but a picture is still a promise made from a distance. Limits ask a more delicate question: \text{What happens as the input gets very close to one point?}

In single-variable calculus, x\to a meant approaching from the left and from the right. In the plane, a point can approach from every direction: along lines, curves, spirals, parabolas, and paths we have not thought to name.

subsection.title: Approaching a point along many paths
subsection.p: Consider the function A(x,y)=\frac{x^2-y^2}{x^2+y^2}, \qquad (x,y)\ne(0,0). The formula is not defined at the origin. But we can ask whether the values settle down as (x,y) approaches (0,0).
Approach along the x-axis. Then y=0, so A(x,0)=\frac{x^2}{x^2}=1 for x\ne0. Along this path, the values approach 1.
Approach along the y-axis. Then x=0, so A(0,y)=\frac{-y^2}{y^2}=-1 for y\ne0. Along this path, the values approach -1.
The same point gives two different limiting answers: 1 \qquad\text{and}\qquad -1. So the function has no limit at the origin.

figure.caption: A two-variable limit must survive every possible approach to the point.

subsection.p: This is the main new issue. A two-variable limit is not a left-right test. It is an all-paths test.

definition.title: Limit of a function of two variables

statement.p: The point (a,b) does not have to be in the domain of f. A limit is about nearby points, not necessarily the point itself.

subsection.p: The phrase whenever (x,y) is sufficiently close means from every direction and along every path. If even one path gives a different answer, the limit fails.

subsection.title: Path tests for nonexistence of a limit
subsection.p: A path is a curve approaching the point. If a limit exists, every path must see the same limiting value.
The quickest way to disprove a limit is to find two paths that disagree.

statement.p: Let B(x,y)=\frac{xy}{x^2+y^2}, \qquad (x,y)\ne(0,0). Along the x-axis, B(x,0)=0. So along that path, the values approach 0.
Along the line y=x, we get B(x,x)=\frac{x^2}{x^2+x^2}=\frac12. So along that path, the values approach \frac12.

subsection.p:
The same function shows something stronger. Along the line y=mx, we get
B(x,mx) \amp= \frac{x(mx)}{x^2+(mx)^2} \amp= \frac{mx^2}{(1+m^2)x^2} \amp= \frac{m}{1+m^2},
for x\ne0. Different slopes m give different limiting values. The origin is not approached by one road; it is approached by infinitely many roads.

statement.p: Let f:D\subseteq\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R. Suppose two paths \mathbf r_1(t)\to(a,b), \qquad \mathbf r_2(t)\to(a,b) as t\to0, and suppose the paths stay in the domain of f except possibly at the endpoint.
If \lim_{t\to0} f(\mathbf r_1(t)) and \lim_{t\to0} f(\mathbf r_2(t)) exist but are different, then \lim_{(x,y)\to(a,b)} f(x,y) does not exist.

proof.p: If the two-variable limit were L, then f(x,y) would approach L whenever (x,y) approaches (a,b). In particular, it would approach L along every path to (a,b).
Two different path limits would force the same limit to equal two different numbers. That is impossible.

subsection.p: The theorem is a tool for showing that a limit does not exist. It is not a tool for proving that a limit exists.
Here is the trap.

example.title: All straight paths agree, but the limit still fails

statement.p:
Define C(x,y)=\frac{x^2y}{x^4+y^2}, \qquad (x,y)\ne(0,0). First approach along a straight line y=mx. If m=0, then C(x,0)=0. If m\ne0, then
C(x,mx) \amp= \frac{x^2(mx)}{x^4+m^2x^2} \amp= \frac{mx^3}{x^2(x^2+m^2)} \amp= \frac{mx}{x^2+m^2}.
As x\to0, \frac{mx}{x^2+m^2}\to0. So every straight-line path gives the limiting value 0.

But now approach along the parabola y=x^2. Then
C(x,x^2) \amp= \frac{x^2(x^2)}{x^4+(x^2)^2} \amp= \frac{x^4}{2x^4} \amp= \frac12.
Along this curved path, the limiting value is \frac12.
So the full limit does not exist, even though every straight-line test gave 0. Straight roads are not enough.

subsection.p: The moral is important:

subsection.title: Polar-coordinate tests near the origin
subsection.p: Near the origin, polar coordinates are built for limits. Every point near (0,0) can be written as x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta, where r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}. Approaching the origin means r\to0. The angle \theta may stay fixed, change, or spin wildly. A good polar argument must work no matter what \theta does.

example.title: A polar-coordinate proof of a limit

statement.p: Let D(x,y)=\frac{x^2y}{x^2+y^2}, \qquad (x,y)\ne(0,0). We want to find \lim_{(x,y)\to(0,0)}D(x,y).

subsection.p: The key was not merely rewriting in polar coordinates. The key was finding a bound that did not depend on \theta.

theorem.title: Polar squeeze test near the origin

statement.p: Suppose f(x,y) is defined near (0,0), except possibly at (0,0). Write x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta. If there is a function g(r) such that |f(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta)-L|\le g(r) for all sufficiently small r\gt0 and all angles \theta, and if \lim_{r\to0^+}g(r)=0, then \boxed{ \lim_{(x,y)\to(0,0)}f(x,y)=L. }

proof.p: The distance from (x,y) to the origin is r. As (x,y)\to(0,0), we have r\to0^+. The inequality |f(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta)-L|\le g(r) holds for every angle, so it controls every possible approach to the origin. Since g(r)\to0, the difference |f(x,y)-L| must also go to 0.

subsection.p: The phrase for all angles \theta is the heart of the theorem. Without it, a path could hide in the angle dependence.

example.title: Polar coordinates can also reveal failure

statement.p:
Return to A(x,y)=\frac{x^2-y^2}{x^2+y^2}. In polar coordinates,
A(x,y) \amp= \frac{r^2\cos^2\theta-r^2\sin^2\theta}{r^2} \amp= \cos^2\theta-\sin^2\theta \amp= \cos(2\theta).
The expression does not depend on r at all. It depends only on the angle.
Along \theta=0, A=1. Along \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}, A=-1. No matter how close we get to the origin, the angle still controls the value. So the limit does not exist.

subsection.p: To use polar coordinates near another point (a,b), shift the point to the origin: u=x-a,\qquad v=y-b. Then write u=r\cos\theta,\qquad v=r\sin\theta. Approaching (a,b) is the same as r=\sqrt{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}\to0.

subsection.title: The formal limit definition in \mathbb R^n
subsection.p: The words nearby inputs give nearby outputs are right, but sometimes we need the exact sentence behind them.
For a function F:D\subseteq\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R^m, both the input and the output may be vectors. Distance measures closeness in both spaces.

statement.p: For every \varepsilon\gt0, there is a \delta\gt0 such that whenever 0\lt\|\mathbf x-\mathbf a\|\lt\delta and \mathbf x\in D, we have \|F(\mathbf x)-\mathbf L\|\lt\varepsilon.

subsection.p: This definition is just the precise version of the all-paths idea. The condition 0\lt\|\mathbf x-\mathbf a\|\lt\delta says that the input is close to \mathbf a, but not equal to \mathbf a. The condition \|F(\mathbf x)-\mathbf L\|\lt\varepsilon says that the output is close to \mathbf L.

example.title: A direct limit proof for a linear function

statement.p:
Let f(x,y)=3x-2y. We claim that \lim_{(x,y)\to(1,4)} f(x,y)=f(1,4)=-5. Compute the difference:
|f(x,y)-(-5)| \amp= |3x-2y+5| \amp= |3(x-1)-2(y-4)|.
Using the triangle inequality, |3(x-1)-2(y-4)| \le 3|x-1|+2|y-4|. Also, |x-1|\le \sqrt{(x-1)^2+(y-4)^2}, and |y-4|\le \sqrt{(x-1)^2+(y-4)^2}. Therefore |f(x,y)-(-5)| \le 5\sqrt{(x-1)^2+(y-4)^2}. If \sqrt{(x-1)^2+(y-4)^2}\lt\delta, then |f(x,y)-(-5)|\lt5\delta. To make this less than \varepsilon, choose \delta=\frac{\varepsilon}{5}. So the formal definition confirms what geometry already suggested: a linear function has no sudden behavior. Its values move continuously with its inputs.

subsection.p: Vector-valued limits work component by component, just as they did for the one-variable vector-valued functions in .

statement.p: Let F:D\subseteq\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R^m be written in components as F(\mathbf x)= \bigl(F_1(\mathbf x),F_2(\mathbf x),\ldots,F_m(\mathbf x)\bigr). Let \mathbf L=(L_1,L_2,\ldots,L_m). Then \lim_{\mathbf x\to\mathbf a}F(\mathbf x)=\mathbf L if and only if \lim_{\mathbf x\to\mathbf a}F_i(\mathbf x)=L_i for every component i=1,2,\ldots,m.

proof.p: If the vector F(\mathbf x) approaches \mathbf L, then the distance between them goes to 0. Each component difference is no larger in absolute value than that distance, so each component approaches its limiting value.

statement.p: Let F(x,y)= \left( x+y,\; \frac{x^2y}{x^2+y^2} \right), \qquad (x,y)\ne(0,0). As (x,y)\to(0,0), x+y\to0. From the polar-coordinate example above, \frac{x^2y}{x^2+y^2}\to0. Therefore \lim_{(x,y)\to(0,0)}F(x,y)=(0,0).

subsection.p: A limit does not care what happens at the point itself. That is both useful and a little dangerous.

For example, define G(x,y)= \begin{cases} \dfrac{x^2y}{x^2+y^2}, & (x,y)\ne(0,0),\\[2mm] 17, & (x,y)=(0,0). \end{cases} The limit at the origin is still 0. But the actual value at the origin is 17. So the surface has a perfectly good limiting height, but the function has placed the point at the wrong height.
That mismatch is exactly what continuity is designed to catch. A limit asks what the nearby values want the answer to be. Continuity asks whether the function actually listens.
